---
title: "Getting Started"
guide-section: "Getting Started"
tags: [Getting Started, Setup]
---

## Motivation


Machine learning for earth observation is powerful but often brittle in operational 
settings: models trained end-to-end on satellite imagery can fail silently when 
presented with new tiles, different sensors, seasonal changes, or unusual 
land-cover mixtures. `seapig` helps manage that uncertainty by letting you make 
selection decisions before acting on model outputs — for example, flagging low-confidence 
predictions for human review or running a more expensive downstream workflow only 
on items that pass a score threshold. The library provides building blocks for 
scoring, calibrating, and integrating selective inference into geospatial 
pipelines so you can reuse the same selection logic across segmentation, classification, 
and regression tasks.


This quickstart uses a deliberately small and readable example ([Landsat-8](https://torchgeo.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api/datasets/landsat.html) 
predictors paired with [Cropland Data Layer](https://torchgeo.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api/datasets/cropland-data-layer.html) 
targets, as implemented in `torchgeo`) to make the core ideas clear. The data 
and code below are chosen for clarity rather than production readiness. The 
general pattern, (1) prepare geospatial train/val/test splits, (2) train a model,
(3) fit a selection score, (4) choose a threshold, and (5) run selective inference, 
is what you can translate to your own data and models.


## A few practical notes

- Simplicity: preprocessing, augmentation, and model configuration are intentionally minimal here. Real projects usually require cloud masking, radiometric correction, tiling strategies, class balancing, and stronger augmentations.
- Reproducibility: fix random seeds and sampling strategies for deterministic runs when needed.
- Transferability: swap the data backend or model and the same selective-inference steps still apply — seapig is modular and task-agnostic.

## Setup

The following code chunk import all required libraries. Note, that
we define a utility script to not further convolute this tutorial
which you would need to download if you want to run the code yourself.

```{python imports}
import os
import tempfile

import torch
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from torchgeo.datasets import CDL, Landsat8, stack_samples
from torchgeo.datasets.utils import download_and_extract_archive
from torchgeo.samplers import GridGeoSampler, RandomGeoSampler
from lightning import Trainer

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import geopandas as gpd


from seapig.scores import CosineScore
from seapig import SelectiveInferenceTask
from seapig.utils import set_backend


from docs.utils import make_split_polygons, MySegmentationTask

# sets progress report backend
set_backend("rich")
# set random seed for reproducibility of the random sampler
torch.manual_seed(20260427)
```

## Data preparation

We download small example predictor and target files and combine them into a 
dataset. The predictors used are coarsened Landsat-8 channels while the target
is represented as a land cover/land use map from the Cropland Data Layer.
Both data sets are used in the [torchgeo tutorials](https://torchgeo.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) 
and are hosted on HuggingFace for easy access. The combined dataset produces 
an image pair that chips can be sampled from and fed into a model.

```{python datasets}
# download example dataset from torchgeo tutorials hosted on huggingface
url = "https://hf.co/datasets/torchgeo/tutorials/resolve/ff30b729e3cbf906148d69a4441cc68023898924/"
landsat_root = os.path.join(tempfile.gettempdir(), "landsat")

# get landsat predictors
landsat8_url = url + "LC08_L2SP_023032_20230831_20230911_02_T1.tar.gz"
download_and_extract_archive(landsat8_url, landsat_root)
landsat8 = Landsat8(paths=landsat_root)

# get cdl targets
cdl_root = os.path.join(tempfile.gettempdir(), "cdl")
cdl_url = url + "2023_30m_cdls.zip"
download_and_extract_archive(cdl_url, cdl_root)
cdl = CDL(paths=cdl_root)

# combine datasets with the & operator to create a new dataset of (predictor, target) pairs
dataset = landsat8 & cdl
```

Next, we create simple spatial train/validation/test splits by slicing the dataset 
extent along the x‑axis. This keeps splits geographically distinct, which is 
important for realistic evaluation of geospatial models. The train split is the 
leftmost 50% of the area, while the validation and test splits are the remaining 
50% divided in half. The `make_split_polygons` function produces polygons that 
define these ROIs based on the dataset bounds and specified proportions.

```{python splits}
# train split: left most up to proportion of the total bounds on the x-axis
# val and test splits: remaining area on the right, split in half on the x-axis
org_bounds = dataset.bounds # tuple(x: slice(xmin, xmax, res), y: slice(ymin, ymax, res), t: slice(tmin, tmax, tres))
size_props = [0.50, 0.25, 0.25] # proportions of the total bounds to use for each split
train_poly, val_poly, test_poly = make_split_polygons(org_bounds, size_props)
```

Here, we visualize the overall extent of the dataset and the train/val/test splits. 
The first plot shows the full area covered by the Landsat-8 scene. The second
plot shows the CDL land cover classes across the area.

```{python plot-aoi}
# plot the complete extent of the dataset
sample = dataset[:]
_ = landsat8.plot(sample)
_ = cdl.plot(sample)
```

The next plot shows the train/val/test splits as red, blue, and green polygons.

```{python plot-splits}
# plot train/val/test splits
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(4, 4))
gpd.GeoSeries(train_poly).plot(ax=ax, edgecolor="red", facecolor="red", label="train", alpha = 0.2)
gpd.GeoSeries(val_poly).plot(ax=ax, edgecolor="blue", facecolor="blue", label="val", alpha = 0.2)
gpd.GeoSeries(test_poly).plot(ax=ax, edgecolor="green", facecolor="green", label="test", alpha = 0.2)
ax.set_title("Train / Val / Test ROIs")
ax.legend()
```

Once we have the spatial extents of our splits ready, we move forward by defining 
samplers for each split. [Samplers](https://torchgeo.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api/samplers.html) 
are used in `torchgeo` to retrieve chips ready for training/evaluation from 
geospatial datasets. For the training split, we use a [`RandomGeoSampler`](https://torchgeo.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api/samplers.html#random-geo-sampler). 
This means that chips with the queried sizes will be randomly sampled from within 
the spatio-temporal extent of the training domain. This may as well include overlaps 
between different samples. We do not wish to have overlaps for the validation and 
testing splits, which is why we are using the [`GridGeoSampler`](https://torchgeo.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api/samplers.html#grid-geo-sampler) 
for those domains. This sampler will produce a grid of non-overlapping chips that 
cover the respective validation and testing domains. The size parameter controls 
the width and height of the sampled chips, while the stride parameter controls 
the spacing between the chips in the grid. The total size of the Landsat-8 scene 
is approx. ~ $1024$ x $1024$ pixels. We use a chip size of $32$ x $32$ pixels, 
which means that a grid-based samplers would produce around $1024 / 32 = 32$ chips 
along each axis, or around 1000 total chips for the entire domain. The [`RandomGeoSampler`](https://torchgeo.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api/samplers.html#random-geo-sampler), 
by default, will produce a number of chips equal to the number a [`GridGeoSampler`](https://torchgeo.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api/samplers.html#grid-geo-sampler)
would produce for the same domain.

```{python samples}
# define samplers for each split
train_sampler = RandomGeoSampler(dataset, roi = train_poly, size=32)
print(f"Number of train samples: {len(train_sampler)}")
val_sampler = GridGeoSampler(dataset, roi = val_poly, size=32)
print(f"Number of val samples: {len(val_sampler)}")
test_sampler = GridGeoSampler(dataset, roi = test_poly, size=32)
print(f"Number of test samples: {len(test_sampler)}")
```

Once we have our samples defined, we move on with defining their respective 
dataloaders. In `PyTorch`, [dataloaders](https://docs.pytorch.org/tutorials/beginner/basics/data_tutorial.html)
are used to produce batches of data from a dataset or sampler. For `torchgeo`, 
these dataloaders need to be adapted to produce batches of chips from geospatial 
datasets and samplers. The `stack_samples` function is used as the collate 
function to produce batched tensors suitable for training from geospatial samples.

```{python loaders}
train_dl = DataLoader(
    dataset, batch_size=4, sampler=train_sampler, collate_fn=stack_samples
)
val_dl = DataLoader(
    dataset, batch_size=4, sampler=val_sampler, collate_fn=stack_samples
)
test_dl = DataLoader(
    dataset, batch_size=4, sampler=test_sampler, collate_fn=stack_samples
)
```

## Model fitting

Once we have our dataloaders ready, we can move on to training a model. The model 
and training configuration here are minimal and meant for demonstration purposes. 
In practice, you would likely want to use a more powerful architecture, stronger 
augmentations, and a longer training schedule. The key point 
is that once you have a trained model, you can fit a selection score on held-out 
data and then apply that score during inference to make selection decisions before 
acting on the model outputs. Here we fit a minimal segmentation task with a 
UNet architecture and a ResNet-18 backbone. The model is trained for 3 epochs 
with a learning rate of 1e-3.

```{python fit-model}
#| warning: false
model = MySegmentationTask(
    model = "unet",
    backbone="resnet18",
    weights=True,
    in_channels=len(landsat8.bands),
    task = "multiclass",
    num_classes=len(cdl.classes),
    loss = "ce",
    lr = 1e-3,
)
trainer = Trainer(max_epochs=3, accelerator="auto")
trainer.fit(model, train_dl, val_dl)
```

## Score calibration

With a trained model in hand, we can now move on to fitting a selection score. 
A selection score is a function that takes in model outputs and produces a score 
that can be used to make selection decisions. The `CosineScore` is used here 
for demonstration purposes, but seapig supports a variety of scores including 
distance- and logit-based scores. The score is expected to produce a low
value for samples that we expect a model to perform well on and a high value for 
samples we expect the model to perform poorly on. The `CosineScore` uses measures
the distance between query embeddings and the training embeddings in the model's latent space. 
The `fit` method computes the necessary statistics from the training and validation 
data to calibrate the score. After fitting, we set a selection threshold at the 
80th percentile of the score distribution on the validation set, meaning that we 
will reject samples that have a score above this threshold from prediction and 
flag them for further review or downstream processing.

```{python fit-score}
score = CosineScore(k = 1)
score.fit(model = model, loaders = {"train": train_dl, "val": val_dl})
score.set_threshold(q=0.80)
print(f"Threshold: {score.threshold}")
```

## Selective inference

We can apply the fitted score to the test set. The `select` method applies the 
score to the model outputs on the test set and produces a selection decision for 
each sample. We can visualize the distribution of scores on the test set and see 
which samples were selected or rejected based on the threshold we set, against the
background of calibration scores from the validation set.

```{python}
test_selection = score.select(model = model, loader = test_dl)
score.plot(query_scores = test_selection["score"])
```

Finally, we can wrap the trained model and fitted score into a `SelectiveInferenceTask`. 
Running `trainer.test` on this wrapped task will compute the usual performance metrics 
on the test set, but differentiate between selected and rejected samples based on the 
score threshold. This allows us to evaluate how well the model performs on the samples 
it is confident about (selected) versus the samples it is uncertain about (rejected), 
giving us insight into the  model's reliability and the effectiveness of the selection 
score in identifying samples that are likely to be mispredicted.

```{python sel-task}
sel_model = SelectiveInferenceTask(task = model, score= score, input_key='image', target_key='mask')
stats = trainer.test(sel_model, test_dl)
```
